**Neath Rugby Football Club** ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): ***Clwb Rygbi Castell-Nedd***) is a Welsh [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") club which plays in the Indigo Premiership for Season 2024/25. The club's home ground is [The Gnoll](/wiki/The_Gnoll "The Gnoll"), [Neath](/wiki/Neath "Neath"). The team is known as the All Blacks because of the team colours: black with only a white [cross pattée](/wiki/Cross_patt%C3%A9e "Cross pattée") as an emblem. They were formed in 1871. They are feeder club to the [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") regional team.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neath_RFC&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neath_RFC&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/The_Gnoll_-_Neath_RFC_-_geograph-2277123.jpg/250px-The_Gnoll_-_Neath_RFC_-_geograph-2277123.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Gnoll_-_Neath_RFC_-_geograph-2277123.jpg)

The Gnoll. Home of Neath RFC

Neath Rugby Football club was established in 1871 by a consortium of ten enthusiasts, their captain at the time, T. P. Whittington would later play international rugby for Scotland in 1873.[[1]](#cite_note-fields-of-praise-1): 24

The club's nickname, 'The Welsh All Blacks', comes from their iconic strip of black jersey, shorts and socks with a white cross pattée. The origin of the team colours is not known for sure. Originally the club's players represented the team in various dark kits and the Cross pattée was introduced by one of their players, thought to have been E.C. Moxham, "to break the monotony". Neath's cross pattée is often incorrectly referred to as a [Maltese Cross](/wiki/Maltese_Cross "Maltese Cross").[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is believed that the strip was later switched to the pure black kit as a mark of respect to player Dick Gordon, who died from injuries sustained on the field of play against [Bridgend RFC](/wiki/Bridgend_Ravens "Bridgend Ravens") in 1880.

On 12 June 1881, eleven teams met in the Castle Hotel, [Neath](/wiki/Neath "Neath") to form what would be accepted as a Welsh rugby union. The founding clubs of the [WFU (Welsh Football Union)](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union"), as it was originally known, were [Swansea C & FC](/wiki/Swansea_RFC "Swansea RFC"), [Pontypool RFC](/wiki/Pontypool_RFC "Pontypool RFC"), [Newport RFC](/wiki/Newport_RFC "Newport RFC"), [Merthyr RFC](/wiki/Merthyr_RFC "Merthyr RFC"), [Llanelli RFC](/wiki/Llanelli_RFC "Llanelli RFC"), [Bangor RFC](/wiki/Bangor_RFC "Bangor RFC"), [Brecon RFC](/wiki/Brecon_RFC "Brecon RFC"), [Cardiff RFC](/wiki/Cardiff_RFC "Cardiff RFC"), [Lampeter College](/wiki/University_of_Wales,_Lampeter "University of Wales, Lampeter"), [Llandovery College](/wiki/Llandovery_College "Llandovery College") and [Llandeilo RFC](/wiki/Llandeilo_RFC "Llandeilo RFC").[[1]](#cite_note-fields-of-praise-1): 24, 41 Strangely Neath RFC was not recorded as being present, even though the meeting took place in the town. It is unknown if this was an oversight by the committee to record the presence of the club, or if Neath RFC did not actually attend. One theory put forward is that the president, [John Llewellyn](/wiki/John_Talbot_Dillwyn_Llewellyn "John Talbot Dillwyn Llewellyn") and secretary, [Sam Clark](/wiki/Samuel_Clark_(rugby_union) "Samuel Clark (rugby union)") of the South Wales Football Union were both Neath men. By attending this new union they would be destroying the SWFU and therefore their own influence in the game. These wounds would soon heal and Neath joined the newly formed WFU in the 1882–83 season and would eventually become pivotal in the union's development, monopolizing the secretaryship from 1896 to 1955.[[1]](#cite_note-fields-of-praise-1): 44–45 Sam Clark would in turn become the first [Welsh](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team") international from Neath RFC, playing in the second Wales game on 28 January 1882. In December 1882 it was decided to amalgamate Neath FC and Neath Abbey FC.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In 1887/88 Neath RFC undertook their first tour of the northern English clubs, including [Hartlepool](/wiki/Hartlepool_Rovers "Hartlepool Rovers"), Manningham and Wortley. The next season Neath played host to Widnes on Christmas Day before undertaking a further northern tour taking on a further five teams in six days. During the 1890/91 season a South West England tour was introduced, which would later become an annual fixture facing clubs such as [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol_Rugby "Bristol Rugby") and [London Welsh](/wiki/London_Welsh_RFC "London Welsh RFC").

Martyn Davies holds a very special place in Welsh rugby history because he was captain of Neath in the club's centenary year of 1971/72 and it was he who became the first captain to hold aloft the Welsh Cup and to then carry it on its now customary "lap of honour" around the old Cardiff Arms Park.

### 2007/08 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neath_RFC&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2007/08 season")]

Their 2007/08 championship season, their fourth in succession, was overshadowed by the death of scrum-half of Gareth Jones, on 16 June 2008. Jones had been injured in a match against [Cardiff RFC](/wiki/Cardiff_RFC "Cardiff RFC") on 20 April.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### 2008/09 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neath_RFC&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2008/09 season")]

Their 2008/09 cup winning season, in which they claimed a victory over Llanelli at the Millennium Stadium, Cardiff. This was the first year since the cup name changed from Konica Minolta Cup to Swalec Cup.

## Club records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neath_RFC&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Club records")]

Neath RFC hold the world record for the number of points (1,917) and tries (345) accumulated in one season (47 Games).[[5]](#cite_note-5) This record was set during the 1988/89 season, it is also worth noting that a try was worth 4 points at the time the record was broken.

The record for the most tries scored in a game in the post-regional era (post 2002) is held by Richard Smith. In January 2010 against Kidwelly RFC Smith scored 7 tries. The pre-regional record of 6 tries is jointly held by [Howie Jones](/wiki/Howie_Jones_(rugby_union) "Howie Jones (rugby union)") (v Aberavon 1928/29), W.D. Williams (v B.P.Llandarcy 1949/50), [Keith Maddocks](/wiki/Keith_Maddocks "Keith Maddocks") (v Ebbw Vale 1956/57), Alan Edmunds (v Cross Keys 1989/90).

## Organisation and finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neath_RFC&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Organisation and finance")]

The company that runs Neath RFC is Neath Rugby Ltd.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/2f/Geraint_Hawkes_disqualification_judgement%2C_High_Court%2C_June_2015.jpg/250px-Geraint_Hawkes_disqualification_judgement%2C_High_Court%2C_June_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Geraint_Hawkes_disqualification_judgement,_High_Court,_June_2015.jpg)

The key lines from the Geraint Hawkes disqualification legal judgement

On 4 June 2015, Geraint Hawkes (Current chairman of Neath RFC and 50% owner of the club) was found to be "unfit to be concerned in the management of a company" by Judge Keyser Q.C. sitting as a Judge of the High Court (see para 68 of the judgement document)[[6]](#cite_note-6) He also decided that the evidence given by Hawkes was "deliberately false" (para 48 - see image).

On 1 July 2015 the judge confirmed the length of the disqualification, as reported by the BBC: "The owner of Neath RFC and his mother have been banned from being company directors for 10 years after failing to declare more than £1.5m in VAT. Geraint Hawkes, 48, and Janis Hawkes, 75, were given the ban at a hearing in Cardiff on Wednesday. Judge Andrew Keyser QC said the pair had been involved in a "serious case" of unpaid VAT. The decision followed a hearing in the High Court Chancery Division sitting in Cardiff last month."[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In an effort to generate more income at their home stadium [The Gnoll](/wiki/The_Gnoll "The Gnoll"), the club announced in late 2008, plans to share it with the town's football club [Neath Athletic](/wiki/Neath_Athletic_A.F.C. "Neath Athletic A.F.C.").[[8]](#cite_note-8) This football team ceased trading at the end of the 2011-12 season due to financial difficulties.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Jardine Norton Ltd became the owners of Neath Rugby Ltd in January 2019.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Club honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neath_RFC&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Club honours")]

* Welsh Club Champions: 1909/10, 1910/11, 1928/29, 1933/34, 1934/35, 1946/47, 1966/67, 1986/87, 1988/89, 1989/90
* [Welsh Premier Division](/wiki/Welsh_Premier_Division "Welsh Premier Division"): 1990/91, 1995/96, 2004/05, 2005/06, 2006/07, 2007/08, 2009/10
* [Welsh Cup](/wiki/WRU_Challenge_Cup "WRU Challenge Cup"): 1971/72, 1988/89, 1989/90, 2003/04, 2007/08, 2008/09
* Welsh Cup Finalists: 1983/84, 1987/88, 1992/93, 1995/96, 2000/01, 2005/06, 2012/13
* [Snelling Sevens](/wiki/Snelling_Sevens "Snelling Sevens") 1964, 1970
* [Principality Premiership Play Off Champions](/w/index.php?title=Principality_Premiership_Play_Off_Champions&action=edit&redlink=1 "Principality Premiership Play Off Champions (page does not exist)"): 2009/2010
* Premiership Cup Winners: 2021/22

## Neath Rugby Supporters Club

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neath_RFC&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Neath Rugby Supporters Club")]

The Supporters Club was restarted at the end of the 2012–13 season, with the aim of providing a link between the fans and the club.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The 2013–14 season saw the first Neath Supporters Hall of Fame, an event intended to take place every season.

2014 inductees:
TP Whittington, Brian Williams, Brian Thomas, Shane Williams, Steve Powell and Dai Morris

2015 inductees:
Rees Stephens, Martyn Davies, Ron Waldron, Kevin Phillips and Gareth Llewellyn
